title: Devāsura — the War of Gods and Demons
subtitle: the eternal churning of light and shadow for the nectar of the deathless

raja.name: Indra
raja.moral: Sovereignty that must be forever re-earned
raja.teaching: Indra wields the vajra and rules the heavens, yet his throne is lost and won again through the ages. Guard the centre of your being — even a god's crown is held only by ceaseless vigilance and dharma.

mantri.name: Brihaspati
mantri.moral: Wisdom is the true power behind the throne
mantri.teaching: Brihaspati counsels the gods across the whole field of heaven. Power without wisdom is a storm without a helm — the sage protects the king before the king conquers.
mantri.fall: When the guru of the gods falls, even the heavens counsel poorly, and the throne trembles.

ratha.name: Divya-Ratha
ratha.teaching: The celestial chariot streaks the sky in a line of light and does not swerve. Commit to your path with a god's certainty — momentum is faith made visible.
ratha.fall: A fallen chariot of heaven teaches that even light, unshielded, can be dimmed.

gaja.name: Airāvata
gaja.teaching: Airavata rose from the churning ocean, vast and radiant, treading its diagonal with serene power. True strength is heaven's — patient, and never cruel.
gaja.fall: The great white elephant falls, and the sky learns that even splendour is not spared the churning.

ashva.name: Uchchaihshravas
ashva.teaching: Uchchaihshravas, the seven-headed horse of the churning, leaps where no straight road runs. When reason halts, a divine daring crosses what none else can.
ashva.fall: A fallen divine steed reminds us that even wonder is mortal in the war of the ages.

padati.name: Deva-Vira
padati.moral: The steadfast light of the humble many
padati.teaching: The deva warrior never steps back, holding the line of heaven one square at a time. The smallest light, unwavering, upholds the whole vault of the sky.
padati.fall: A celestial warrior falls so the line of light may hold — no radiance spent for dharma is ever lost.

raja.name: Bali
raja.moral: Even shadow may hold a noble heart
raja.teaching: Bali, king of the asuras, was mighty, generous, and true to his word — a demon more virtuous than many gods. Judge not by the side, but by the soul; the centre you must guard is your integrity.
raja.fall: When noble Bali falls, the heavens themselves fall silent, unsure they deserved the win.

mantri.name: Shukrāchārya
mantri.moral: Genius devoted, for good or ill
mantri.teaching: Shukracharya alone knew the sanjivani, the art of reviving the fallen, and ranged the whole field to serve his kings. The sharpest wisdom is a blade — its worth is decided by the hand and the cause it serves.
mantri.fall: When the asura guru falls, no art remains to raise the demons' dead, and the tide turns.

ratha.name: Asura-Ratha
ratha.teaching: The asura chariot charges without doubt or turning. Certainty is strength — but certainty in a shadowed cause only reaches its ruin the faster.
ratha.fall: A shattered asura chariot warns that force, however proud, is not the same as right.

gaja.name: Asura-Gaja
gaja.moral: Vast might in a hungry cause
gaja.teaching: The asura elephant is a mountain of dark power, treading its diagonal with terrible weight. Might is not wrong in itself — but might that serves only hunger devours its own bearer.
gaja.fall: The dark titan falls, and the field learns that no mountain of might can outweigh the balance of dharma.

ashva.name: Asura-Ashva
ashva.teaching: The asura steed leaps from darkness where none expect it. Cleverness is a gift of both gods and demons — unlit by conscience, it wounds its own rider first.
ashva.fall: A fallen demon-steed reminds us that cunning, unmoored from truth, is still mortal.

padati.name: Asura-Vira
padati.moral: The valour of the misled many
padati.teaching: The asura warrior never retreats, marching bravely for his king. Courage is real in every heart — the question is only the banner over it, so choose a cause worthy of your fire.
padati.fall: An asura falls for the shadow-cause — no soldier's bravery is small, though the banner may be.

pawnTeachings: The light holds the line one square at a time — heaven is upheld by the humble, not the high.
A single unwavering ray outlasts a passing storm of shadow.
Serve the good without pride; the smallest deva guards the whole sky.
Hold the line beside your kin — a wall of light outshines any lone star.
The first step into the war of ages is duty; the rest is only holding fast.
Fear the churning, yet churn — for the nectar rises only when you dare the poison.
You cannot un-shine a light once given; so spend your radiance on what is worthy.
A warrior of heaven guards more than a square — he guards the balance of the worlds.
The sacrifice of the small keeps the great wheel turning toward the deathless.
Fight without hatred, even the asura — for gods and demons churn one ocean together.
pawnTeachingsDark: The asura never yields — but ask always whose deathless nectar your fall will buy.
A dark heart can be brave; give that bravery to a cause that deserves it.
One demon marches for his king's pride; weigh always whose pride your steps uphold.
Might offered to hunger raises a throne that will one day fall upon its builder.
Hold the line for your kin — yet a wall for shadow cannot stand against the dawn.
The first step into a shadowed war is the hardest to unmake; take it with open eyes.
You cannot un-march a step — so be certain of the banner you follow into the dark.
A demon guards the gate, unaware the truest foe is the hunger within his own king.
The sacrifice of the misled is the heaviest of all — spend yourself for the light.
Serve, but never blindly — even a foot-soldier answers for the cause he carries.
captureLines: Gods and demons churned one ocean; every blow between them stirred both poison and nectar.
A shadow falls before the light — yet do not gloat, for the wheel of the ages turns for both.
A piece falls and the churning advances; attachment to the winning is the only true loss.
Strike for dharma, never for spite — the asura, too, is a face of the one cosmic order.
Even Indra bowed to noble Bali — honour the foe whose soul outshone his side.
captureLinesDark: The asuras strike like the churning depths — yet every blow for hunger recoils on the striker.
A god falls to the demon host; even in the shadow's triumph, mourn the light you dim.
The shadow gains a square, and the ocean churns darker; conquest is a debt, not a gain.
Strike for your king if you must, but know whose deathless thirst your sword truly serves.
Even the asuras were valiant — valour spent for hunger is the saddest churning of all.
promotionLine: The steadfast Deva-Vira, having crossed the whole field of heaven, is raised to a celestial counsellor. Radiance carried to its end becomes wisdom.
checkLine: Indra is threatened — rally the host of heaven and shield the throne of the gods.
checkmateTitle: Amrita — the Deathless Won for the Gods
checkmateLine: The gods drink the nectar and the heavens hold. Reflect: the deathless is not a prize to seize but a balance to keep — light must forever answer for the shadow it defeats.
promotionLineDark: A steadfast Asura-Vira, having crossed the whole field, is raised to a lord of the shadow-host. Yet nectar seized by hunger turns to poison in the drinking.
checkLineDark: Bali is threatened — the asura host closes ranks to shield their noble demon-emperor.
checkmateLineDark: The asuras seize the nectar for a moment. Yet reflect: what is won by hunger cannot be held — the churning will turn again, as it forever must.
